subject: Statistics
title: Election Forecasting
description: A quick, practical guide to Election Forecasting.

hook.text: FiveThirtyEight gave Trump a 29% chance in 2016. He won. Was the forecast wrong? No — 29% happens.

buildup.text: Election forecasts aggregate polls, adjust for biases, and simulate thousands of possible outcomes.

discovery.text: A forecast is a probability distribution, not a prediction. 70% for Clinton meant 30% for Trump.

twist.text: People interpret 70% as certainty. Communicating probabilistic forecasts to the public remains a huge challenge.

climax.text: Correlated errors across states matter most. If polls are biased in Ohio, they're likely biased in Pennsylvania too.

punchline.text: 29% is not zero. People confuse unlikely with impossible.

quiz.question: Why wasn't the 2016 FiveThirtyEight forecast 'wrong' even though Trump won?
options: It gave Trump a 29% chance — unlikely events with that probability occur about 3 in 10 times
It actually predicted Trump would win
The forecast was for a different election
